narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 67.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from key aspects of your profile, like your low Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings in school—placing you in the bottom 10% and 20% respectively, well below the typical student's mid-range performance—along with your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 31, both lower than the averages of 3.21 and 36.70. You come from an upper-middle-income family, plan to study full-time like most applicants, and as a Black male, you're in underrepresented groups compared to the mostly White, full-time student pool.

The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your Year 3 decile rank, which had the greatest pull toward predicting bar failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, your LSAT score, and your racial background. Each of these top factors nudged the prediction toward failure rather than success, with your weaker early academic deciles standing out as the primary drivers since they signal challenges in sustaining strong performance. Your LSAT and undergraduate GPA came next in influence, reinforcing concerns about foundational skills needed for the bar, while your family income, full-time status, and gender had progressively smaller roles, still tilting slightly against you.

In comparison to typical students who pass, your lower deciles and test scores put you at a clear disadvantage, as the model sees these as the strongest predictors of bar struggles. While demographics like race played a supporting role—given Black students make up just 4.6% of the dataset and face steeper odds—your academic metrics dominated the reasoning.

To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 36, as scores are improvable with targeted prep, and build a stronger academic record in any upcoming programs to lift those decile rankings. Strengthening study habits early could mimic the paths of successful students from similar backgrounds. Consider bar exam prep courses tailored for first-time success, and reapply once you've shored up these areas—many have turned predictions around this way. You're capable of changing this trajectory; keep pushing forward.
